meta.title: Huaqiangbei
meta.subtitle: Building & floor directory
meta.tagline: The world's largest electronics market is not one place — it's ~20 vertical malls, each owning a category. This is the building-by-building map, on real coordinates.
meta.methodology: Building locations are real coordinates from Google Places, converted from China's GCJ-02 datum to WGS-84 so they sit correctly on the OpenStreetMap / CARTO basemap. Built in the spirit of Andrew “bunnie” Huang's classic map but drawn fresh; it does not reproduce his work. Pins are accurate to the building; floor details are approximate and booth-level vendor lists are omitted because they change too fast to trust.

tiers.name: Anchor towers
tiers.blurb: If you only work four buildings, these are them.

tiers.name: Phones & mobile parts
tiers.blurb: Where you can assemble a phone from loose parts.

tiers.name: Components & broad-line
tiers.blurb: Parts, computers, and one-stop export markets.

tiers.name: Niche & specialist
tiers.blurb: Single-category buildings worth a dedicated trip.

buildings.name: SEG Plaza
buildings.hanzi: 赛格电子市场 / 赛格广场
buildings.floors: Market on the lower floors (≈1–10); upper floors are trading-company offices
buildings.note: The component heart of the district and its main landmark — ICs, PCBs, passives, connectors, batteries, power supplies, tools. The ground floor leans components and small digital. Start here.

buildings.name: SEG Electronics Market
buildings.note: The original booth-style component market next to the tower — five floors of parts and gadgets, bargain everything. With SEG Plaza this is the deepest component pool in the district.

buildings.name: Huaqiang Electronic World
buildings.note: The more modern face of HQB — proper storefronts rather than booths, skewing to finished goods and consumer electronics. Strong for accessories, audio, LED, assembled goods, and second-hand computers.

buildings.name: Mingtong Digital City
buildings.note: The big caveat: Mingtong has pivoted heavily to imported beauty and cosmetics. Phone screens, batteries and repair parts still trade here, but expect roughly half the building to be cosmetics now.

buildings.name: Yuanwang Digital Mall
buildings.floors: Floors 1–2 phones, parts, chargers, cases, screens, batteries · Floor 3 bulk and refurbished phones with on-site repair · cameras, laptops and security gear higher up
buildings.note: The mobile-phone epicenter — wholesale parts, screen replacements, and used/refurbished phones graded by condition. The district's main phone-repair and second-hand hub.

buildings.name: Tongtiandi Telecom Market
buildings.floors: Top floor finished phones · middle floors MacBooks, iPads, watches, batteries · lower floors iPhone components. Cash, little English, closes ~8:30pm
buildings.note: The big second-hand iPhone market — every part to rebuild a phone, plus finished devices traded in bulk.

buildings.name: Longsheng Phone Wholesale Center
buildings.note: A dedicated mobile-phone and accessories wholesale center in the northern phone cluster.

buildings.name: Hualianfa Building
buildings.note: Wholesale of the latest finished phones — iPhone, Xiaomi, Huawei, Samsung and the domestic brands.

buildings.name: Manha Commercial Plaza
buildings.note: Second-hand phones and assorted wholesale goods, next to Yuanwang in the northern cluster.

buildings.name: Yiyitong Phone Accessories
buildings.note: Phone parts and accessories beside the metro — cases, cables, spares of every kind.

buildings.name: Shenzhen International Electronics City
buildings.floors: F1 branded phones, security, comms, appliances, repair · F2 stage audio, cameras, game consoles, computers
buildings.note: Smaller but broad — a good single stop for pro-audio, cameras and a mix of categories.

buildings.name: Baohua Building
buildings.note: Computer and digital products from ISO9001-certified vendors; tidy underground shops.

buildings.name: Saibo Digital Plaza
buildings.note: Communications, electronic and digital goods across many brands; cheap and broad.

buildings.name: Xinyazhou Electronics Market
buildings.note: Electronic components with one-stop service including customs clearance, tax and logistics — useful for export buyers.

buildings.name: Pacific Security Market
buildings.floors: Basement–4F now phone cases · 5–6F security & surveillance
buildings.note: Once the pure security market; today its lower floors are wall-to-wall phone cases (a major bulk-buy source) with CCTV, cameras and access control pushed up to floors 5–6.

buildings.name: Duhui Electronic City
buildings.note: A more modern mall on the western edge with business, storage and transport services bundled in.

orientation.v: Metro Line 1, 2 or 7 to Huaqiangbei Station. Exit C is closest to SEG Plaza; Exit D is closest to Mingtong and Huaqiang Electronic World. About 10 minutes from the Futian / Hong Kong border crossing.
SEG Plaza and the SEG Electronics Market first — ICs, PCBs, passives, connectors, tools.
Huaqiang Electronic World; Saibo for cheap broad-line.
Yuanwang and the northern cluster (Longsheng, Manha); Tongtiandi for iPhone parts.
Pacific Security's lower floors — now a phone-case bazaar.
Pacific Security (floors 5–6); cameras also at International Electronics City.
Work one or two buildings per day. Trying to cover everything in a day is how you see nothing.

notes: Building and floor are the durable unit. Individual booths churn constantly — the SEG component floor is stable; the specific vendor on it next year may not be.
The grey-market and second-hand phone trade runs openly in the northern buildings. Verify provenance and IMEI on anything sold as new, sealed, or pre-owned.
Prices for obvious foreign visitors get marked up. A calculator, a target price, and willingness to walk are the whole negotiation.
The line between the physical market and online (1688, Pinduoduo, livestream) has blurred — many booths are now fulfilment fronts for online stores.
